fr:title.text: path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:title.text: path objects and the univalence axiom › Reflexive graphs (temporary section)

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: path objects and the univalence axiom › Reflexive graphs (temporary section)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed reflexive graph over \mathcal {A}. The total reflexive graph of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A}, written \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B}, is defined as follows:

fr:title.text: the discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two reflexive graphs. We define the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:

fr:title.text: product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:title.text: the intrinsic path object on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The discrete reflexive graph on a type A is a path object, which we shall refer to as the intrinsic path object on A.

fr:title.text: the intrinsic path object on a type › forest://erct/erct-0013/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the intrinsic path object on a type › forest://erct/erct-0013/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:title.text: the univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:title.text: dependent product of contractible types › forest://erct/erct-001A/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: dependent product of contractible types › forest://erct/erct-001A/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.
The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.

fr:title.text: Dependent product of path objects › forest://erct/erct-001C/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Dependent product of path objects › forest://erct/erct-001C/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} is a path object.

fr:title.text: binary product of path objects › forest://erct/erct-003E/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: binary product of path objects › forest://erct/erct-003E/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The univalence property of \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

Let \chi _x \colon a = x be the witness of contraction for any x:A. Given b : Ba and x:A, we define \mathsf {ind}_{A,a}\,b\,x : Bx by transporting b along the identification \chi _a^{-1} \cdot \chi _x \colon a = x:

fr:title.text: path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:title.text: path objects and the univalence axiom › Reflexive graphs (temporary section)

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: path objects and the univalence axiom › Reflexive graphs (temporary section)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed reflexive graph over \mathcal {A}. The total reflexive graph of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A}, written \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B}, is defined as follows:

fr:title.text: the discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two reflexive graphs. We define the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:

fr:title.text: product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:title.text: the intrinsic path object on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The discrete reflexive graph on a type A is a path object, which we shall refer to as the intrinsic path object on A.

fr:title.text: the intrinsic path object on a type › forest://erct/erct-0013/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the intrinsic path object on a type › forest://erct/erct-0013/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:title.text: the univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:title.text: dependent product of contractible types › forest://erct/erct-001A/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: dependent product of contractible types › forest://erct/erct-001A/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.
The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.

fr:title.text: Dependent product of path objects › forest://erct/erct-001C/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Dependent product of path objects › forest://erct/erct-001C/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} is a path object.

fr:title.text: binary product of path objects › forest://erct/erct-003E/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: binary product of path objects › forest://erct/erct-003E/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The univalence property of \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

html:ol.html:li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
left-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
right-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased right- and left-cloven representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.
We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj}\,\mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

fr:title.text: the total path object of a displayed path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the total path object of a displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B} is a path object.

fr:title.text: the total path object of a displayed path object › forest://erct/erct-0018/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the total path object of a displayed path object › forest://erct/erct-0018/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: the intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord {\vartriangle }A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.
We next show how right-cloven and left-cloven families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base.

fr:title.text: display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of left-cloven families of path objects

html:ol.html:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be p_*b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} b';

fr:title.text: display of left-cloven families of path objects › forest://erct/erct-0033/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of left-cloven families of path objects › forest://erct/erct-0033/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each \mathcal {B} x.

fr:title.text: display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of right-cloven families of path objects

html:ol.html:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} p^*b';

fr:title.text: display of right-cloven families of path objects › forest://erct/erct-0034/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of right-cloven families of path objects › forest://erct/erct-0034/0

fr:title.text: covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:title.text: contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:title.text: path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: path object structure on reflexive graphs › forest://erct/erct-0021/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: path object structure on reflexive graphs › forest://erct/erct-0021/0

html:ol.html:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:title.text: the standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon s\sim s'\colon B\to A and a witness h\colon f\circ s' \sim 1_{B} that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} that s is a section of f as follows:

fr:title.text: the standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:

fr:title.text: further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:title.text: dependent sum of contractible types › forest://erct/erct-003V/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: dependent sum of contractible types › forest://erct/erct-003V/0

fr:title.text: dependent sum of n-truncated types › forest://erct/erct-003W/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: dependent sum of n-truncated types › forest://erct/erct-003W/0

fr:title.text: dependent product of n-truncated types › forest://erct/erct-003Z/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: dependent product of n-truncated types › forest://erct/erct-003Z/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.

fr:title.text: being contractible is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-001N/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being contractible is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-001N/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from Lemma and Lemma .

fr:title.text: being a proposition is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being a proposition is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is a proposition.

fr:title.text: being a proposition is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-001O/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being a proposition is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-001O/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is n-truncated.

fr:title.text: being n-truncated is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-001P/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being n-truncated is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-001P/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We proceed by induction on the truncation level n.

html:ol.html:li: When n=-2, we recall from Lemma that being contractible is a proposition.
When n=-1, we recall from Lemma that being a proposition is a proposition.

fr:title.text: being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That a given map f\colon A \to B is n-truncated is a proposition.

fr:title.text: being an n-truncated map is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-001U/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being an n-truncated map is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-001U/0

fr:title.text: being a surjection is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being a surjection is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That a given map f\colon E\to B is surjective is a proposition.

fr:title.text: being a surjection is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-002B/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being a surjection is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-002B/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By Corollary .
Any invertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a section.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a retraction r\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. To construct h_r\colon r\circ f\sim 1_{A} exhibiting r as a retraction of f, we use the identification between any two elements of the fiber of f over fx.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a retraction.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a section s\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. The witness h_s\colon f\circ s\sim 1_{B} exhibiting s as a section of f, we use the second component of the centre of contraction.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a biinvertible.
Because contractible maps are both sections and retractions.
Any quasi-invertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
Any quasi-inverse can be improved to a coherent inverse, and any coherently invertible map is contractible.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.

The section and retraction are built using by postcomposing with the designated section and retraction of f; the witnesses for the postcomposition sections and retractions are constructed using the univalence principle for function spaces.
Analogous to the corresponding result for pushforward along biinvertible maps.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A \to B is propositional.
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is surjective.
This is immediate, using the unit map of propositional truncation and the section of f.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is a bijection.
By Lemma and Corollary .
Any embedding f\colon A\to B is propositional.
Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0=\mathsf {act}_{f}\phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\phi = p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.
Any propositional map f\colon A\to B is an embedding.

Fixing x,y:A we must show that \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x = y \to fx=fy is invertible. By Lemma and Lemma , it suffices to exhibit a biinverse to \mathsf {act}_{f} (it is convenient that the underlying maps of the section and retraction need not be identical).
Any biinvertible map is an embedding.
By Lemma and Lemma .
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is contractible.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is propositional by Lemma , the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is biinvertible.
By Lemma , bijections are contractible; by Corollary , bijections are contractible.

fr:title.text: coherence of different notions of equivalence

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: coherence of different notions of equivalence

fr:title.text: coherence of different notions of equivalence › coherence of biinverses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: coherence of different notions of equivalence › coherence of biinverses

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon A\to B be a section of some r\colon B\to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

fr:title.text: sections of sections are unique › forest://erct/erct-002Q/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: sections of sections are unique › forest://erct/erct-002Q/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f\colon A\to B is a retract of the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B}, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By Lemma , it suffices to show that the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B} is a proposition, which follows via Lemma and Lemma .
Let f\colon A\to B be a retraction of some s\colon B\to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.

fr:title.text: retractions of retractions are unique › forest://erct/erct-002R/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: retractions of retractions are unique › forest://erct/erct-002R/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of -\circ f at 1_{A}.
The type of biinverses of a given function f\colon A\to B is a proposition.

fr:title.text: being biinvertible is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-002S/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being biinvertible is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-002S/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Sections of sections and retractions of retractions are unique.

fr:title.text: coherence of different notions of equivalence › coherence of inverses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: coherence of different notions of equivalence › coherence of inverses

fr:title.text: the coherent remainder of a section

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the coherent remainder of a section

fr:title.text: coherent remainder as a path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: coherent remainder as a path object

html:li.html:p: We exhibit the latter as a right-cloven family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0,\eta _1\colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A} and \eta _{01}\colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^*\colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _1\sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _0\sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

fr:mainmatter.html:p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:

fr:title.text: the coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:title.text: the coherent remainder of a section is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-0043/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the coherent remainder of a section is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-0043/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By Lemma , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in Example :
It is not difficult to show using Definition that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.
For any function f\colon A\to B, the type of inverses to f is a proposition.

fr:title.text: being invertible is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-0044/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being invertible is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-0044/0

fr:title.text: being an embedding is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being an embedding is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That a given function f\colon A \to B is an embedding is a proposition.

fr:title.text: being an embedding is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-0047/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being an embedding is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-0047/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By Corollary and Corollary .
That a given map f\colon A \to B is bijective is a proposition.

fr:title.text: being bijective is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-003G/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: being bijective is a proposition › forest://erct/erct-003G/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By Lemma , Lemma via Lemma .

html:ol.html:li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
left-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
right-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased right- and left-cloven representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.
We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj}\,\mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

fr:title.text: the total path object of a displayed path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the total path object of a displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B} is a path object.

fr:title.text: the total path object of a displayed path object › forest://erct/erct-0018/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the total path object of a displayed path object › forest://erct/erct-0018/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: the intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord {\vartriangle }A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.
We next show how right-cloven and left-cloven families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base.

fr:title.text: display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of left-cloven families of path objects

html:ol.html:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be p_*b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} b';

fr:title.text: display of left-cloven families of path objects › forest://erct/erct-0033/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of left-cloven families of path objects › forest://erct/erct-0033/0

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each \mathcal {B} x.

fr:title.text: display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of right-cloven families of path objects

html:ol.html:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} p^*b';

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of right-cloven families of path objects › forest://erct/erct-0034/0

fr:title.text: path object structure on reflexive graphs › forest://erct/erct-0021/0

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: path object structure on reflexive graphs › forest://erct/erct-0021/0

html:ol.html:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:title.text: the standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon s\sim s'\colon B\to A and a witness h\colon f\circ s' \sim 1_{B} that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} that s is a section of f as follows:

fr:title.text: the standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: the standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:
